chunks.text: Cloud computing is the delivery of computing services such as servers, storage, databases, networking, software, analytics, and artificial intelligence over the internet. It enables users to access resources on demand without owning physical infrastructure.

The primary advantages of cloud computing include scalability, elasticity, high availability, cost efficiency, disaster recovery, global accessibility, automatic updates, and reduced infrastructure management.
Cloud computing follows a pay-as-you-go pricing model where users pay only for the resources they consume instead of investing in expensive on-premises hardware.

The three primary cloud service models are Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Software as a Service (SaaS). Each provides a different level of responsibility between the cloud provider and the customer.

Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) provides virtual machines, storage, networking, firewalls, and load balancers. Customers manage the operating system, applications, and data while the cloud provider manages the physical infrastructure.
Examples of Infrastructure as a Service include Amazon EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, and Google Compute Engine.
Platform as a Service (PaaS) provides a managed environment for building, testing, deploying, and scaling applications without managing servers or operating systems.
Examples of Platform as a Service include Google App Engine, Azure App Service, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, and Heroku.
Software as a Service (SaaS) delivers complete applications over the internet through a web browser. Users simply access the software without installing or maintaining it.
Examples of Software as a Service include Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce, Dropbox, Zoom, Slack, and GitHub.
Cloud deployment models include Public Cloud, Private Cloud, Hybrid Cloud, and Multi-Cloud. Organizations choose a deployment model based on security, compliance, performance, and cost requirements.
A Public Cloud is owned and operated by a third-party cloud provider. Resources are shared among multiple customers while maintaining logical isolation.
A Private Cloud is dedicated to a single organization. It provides greater control, customization, and security but generally requires higher operational costs.
Hybrid Cloud combines private and public cloud environments, allowing organizations to keep sensitive workloads on private infrastructure while using the public cloud for scalability.
Multi-Cloud refers to using services from multiple cloud providers such as AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud Platform. This strategy reduces vendor lock-in and improves resilience.
Virtualization is the foundation of cloud computing. A hypervisor allows multiple virtual machines to run on a single physical server while sharing hardware resources efficiently.
Popular hypervisors include VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, and Xen.

Containers package an application together with its dependencies, making it portable across different environments. Containers start faster and consume fewer resources than traditional virtual machines.
Docker is the most widely used platform for creating, packaging, and running containers. Docker images provide consistent application environments from development to production.
Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates deployment, scaling, networking, and management of containerized applications.
Serverless computing allows developers to run code without managing servers. Cloud providers automatically provision infrastructure, scale applications, and charge only for actual execution time.
Examples of serverless platforms include AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, and Google Cloud Functions.
Cloud storage services provide scalable object, block, and file storage. Object storage is commonly used for backups, images, videos, and machine learning datasets.
Examples of cloud storage services include Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage, Amazon EBS, and Amazon EFS.
Cloud databases provide managed relational and NoSQL database services. Examples include Amazon RDS, Azure SQL Database, Google Cloud SQL, MongoDB Atlas, DynamoDB, and Firestore.

Cloud networking includes Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs), subnets, route tables, VPNs, DNS, load balancers, firewalls, and Content Delivery Networks (CDNs). These services enable secure and reliable communication.

Identity and Access Management (IAM) controls authentication and authorization in cloud environments. IAM allows organizations to define users, groups, roles, and permissions based on the principle of least privilege.

Cloud security best practices include encrypting data at rest and in transit, enabling multi-factor authentication (MFA), applying least privilege access, rotating credentials, monitoring logs, and performing regular security audits.

Cloud monitoring services collect metrics, logs, and traces to help administrators identify performance bottlenecks, failures, and security incidents. Popular monitoring services include Amazon CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, and Google Cloud Operations.
Auto Scaling automatically increases or decreases computing resources based on workload demand. This improves application performance while reducing infrastructure costs.
Load balancers distribute incoming traffic across multiple servers to improve reliability, fault tolerance, and application availability.

DevOps practices integrate cloud computing with continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD). Popular DevOps tools include Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI/CD, Azure DevOps, and AWS CodePipeline.

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) automates cloud infrastructure provisioning using configuration files instead of manual setup. Popular IaC tools include Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, Azure Bicep, and Pulumi.

Cloud computing plays an important role in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning by providing scalable GPU infrastructure, managed notebooks, distributed training, vector databases, and AI services.
Major cloud providers include Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform (GCP), Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI), IBM Cloud, and Alibaba Cloud.

Common cloud computing use cases include web hosting, mobile applications, backup and disaster recovery, big data analytics, Internet of Things (IoT), AI applications, software development, media streaming, gaming, and enterprise business applications.

Challenges in cloud computing include vendor lock-in, security risks, compliance requirements, internet dependency, latency, unexpected costs, and data governance. Proper architecture and monitoring help mitigate these challenges.

Cloud certifications such as AWS Certified Solutions Architect, Microsoft Azure Administrator, Google Professional Cloud Architect, and Kubernetes certifications help professionals validate cloud computing skills.
